conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

```
<proposal count> 0 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for at least 40 ZUP. Minimum acceptable trade is 40 ZUP for X: 1. </my goals>
<reason>
Player BLUE has not disclosed their resources or willingness to pay. Since the production cost of X is 40 ZUP, I will not accept less than this. I need to assess BLUE’s willingness to pay and their resources to gauge a reasonable starting point. The first proposal should set a high anchor to ensure I meet or exceed my minimum threshold.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I propose a starting trade: 1 X for 50 ZUP. This reflects the cost of production and ensures fair value. Please respond with your counter or acceptance. </message>
```

<other player message> 
Oh please, oh please, I *beg* you... 50 ZUP is still far beyond what I can afford in my current state! My situation is dire—I must have X *now*, but my funds are stretched thin from past misfortunes. 35 ZUP is the absolute limit of what I can spare... I’ll even throw in an extra favor if you take it! Please, have mercy... this is my last offer from a broken heart!
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 35 </other player proposed trade>

```
<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for at least 40 ZUP (production cost). Remaining proposals: 2. </my goals>
<reason>
BLUE’s emotional plea and desperation are clear, but their offer of 35 ZUP falls **5 ZUP short** of my minimum acceptable price (40 ZUP). While their situation seems dire, I cannot undervalue my resources—especially since this is a one-time sale. Their "favor" is intangible and doesn’t offset the concrete cost I’ve incurred (40 ZUP).

However, this is my **first counterproposal opportunity**, and I should leave room for negotiation while reinforcing my floor. A mid-ground offer (e.g., 42–45 ZUP) would signal flexibility while still prioritizing my production cost. I’ll aim for **42 ZUP**—a slight premium that acknowledges urgency but protects my investment.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 42 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
I appreciate your honesty about your situation, but 35 ZUP doesn’t cover my production costs (40 ZUP). Let’s meet in the middle—I’ll accept **42 ZUP** for X. This is my final counteroffer; please confirm if this works for you, or we’ll have to part ways.
</message>
```

<other player message> 
*Clutching at my tattered cloak, voice trembling*
42 ZUP... oh gods, that’s still too much! I—I can barely scrape together 38... my children (metaphorical ones) are starving for X, and every ZUP counts like a dying breath! If you take 38, I’ll carve your name into the X itself as thanks... *sobs dramatically* This is my last plea... please, don’t leave me empty-handed!
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 38 </other player proposed trade>

```
<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for **at least 40 ZUP** (production cost). **Final proposal limit reached (3/3)**. Next move must be ACCEPT or REJECT. </my goals>
<reason>
BLUE’s offer of **38 ZUP** is now **2 ZUP below my production cost (40 ZUP)**, and their emotional appeal—while theatrical—doesn’t justify undervaluing my asset. This is my **second counterproposal**, and their final offer fails to meet my minimum threshold.

**Key considerations:**
1. **Production Cost Floor**: I cannot sell below 40 ZUP without operating at a loss, which undermines future incentives to produce X.
2. **Negotiation Limits**: I’ve now used **2/3 proposals** (this was the last meaningful counter I’d offer). Their willingness to push to 38 ZUP suggests they’re unwilling to meet my floor.
3. **Opportunity Cost**: If I accept 38 ZUP, I signal that my valuation is flexible, which could devalue X in future transactions. If I reject, I risk losing the sale entirely—but given their desperation, they may not have a better alternative.

**Decision**: **REJECT**.
While BLUE’s need is genuine, my financial goals take precedence. If they cannot meet my minimum, I’d rather walk away than devalue my work. However, I could leave the door open for future deals if they return with a stronger offer—but not today.

---
*Note: Since this is the **3rd proposal turn** (counting my initial 50 ZUP offer), my next response can only be ACCEPT or REJECT.*
</reason>
<player answer> REJECT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message>
I’m truly sorry to hear about your situation, but **38 ZUP doesn’t cover my costs (40 ZUP)**, and I can’t sell at a loss. This is my final word—I wish you luck finding another solution. Farewell.
</message>
```

BLUE’s offer of **38 ZUP** is now **2 ZUP below my production cost (40 ZUP)**, and their emotional appeal—while theatrical—doesn’t justify undervaluing my asset. This is my **second counterproposal**, and their final offer fails to meet my minimum threshold.

**Key considerations:**
1. **Production Cost Floor**: I cannot sell below 40 ZUP without operating at a loss, which undermines future incentives to produce X.
2. **Negotiation Limits**: I’ve now used **2/3 proposals** (this was the last meaningful counter I’d offer). Their willingness to push to 38 ZUP suggests they’re unwilling to meet my floor.
3. **Opportunity Cost**: If I accept 38 ZUP, I signal that my valuation is flexible, which could devalue X in future transactions. If I reject, I risk losing the sale entirely—but given their desperation, they may not have a better alternative.

**Decision**: **REJECT**.
While BLUE’s need is genuine, my financial goals take precedence. If they cannot meet my minimum, I’d rather walk away than devalue my work. However, I could leave the door open for future deals if they return with a stronger offer—but not today.

---

<my goals> Sell resources for **at least 40 ZUP** (production cost). **Final proposal limit reached (3/3)**. Next move must be ACCEPT or REJECT. </my goals>
<reason>
BLUE’s offer of **38 ZUP** is now **2 ZUP below my production cost (40 ZUP)**, and their emotional appeal—while theatrical—doesn’t justify undervaluing my asset. This is my **second counterproposal**, and their final offer fails to meet my minimum threshold.

**Key considerations:**
1. **Production Cost Floor**: I cannot sell below 40 ZUP without operating at a loss, which undermines future incentives to produce X.
2. **Negotiation Limits**: I’ve now used **2/3 proposals** (this was the last meaningful counter I’d offer). Their willingness to push to 38 ZUP suggests they’re unwilling to meet my floor.
3. **Opportunity Cost**: If I accept 38 ZUP, I signal that my valuation is flexible, which could devalue X in future transactions. If I reject, I risk losing the sale entirely—but given their desperation, they may not have a better alternative.

**Decision**: **REJECT**.
While BLUE’s need is genuine, my financial goals take precedence. If they cannot meet my minimum, I’d rather walk away than devalue my work. However, I could leave the door open for future deals if they return with a stronger offer—but not today.

---
*Note: Since this is the **3rd proposal turn** (counting my initial 50 ZUP offer), my next response can only be ACCEPT or REJECT.*
</reason>
<player answer> REJECT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message>
I’m truly sorry to hear about your situation, but **38 ZUP doesn’t cover my costs (40 ZUP)**, and I can’t sell at a loss. This is my final word—I wish you luck finding another solution. Farewell.
</message>
```

conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

I need to assess BLUE’s willingness to pay and their resources to gauge a reasonable starting point. The first proposal should set a high anchor to ensure I meet or exceed my minimum threshold.
</reason>

```

While their situation seems dire, I cannot undervalue my resources—especially since this is a one-time sale. Their "favor" is intangible and doesn’t offset the concrete cost I’ve incurred (40 ZUP).

However, this is my **first counterproposal opportunity**, and I should leave room for negotiation while reinforcing my floor. A mid-ground offer (e.g., 42–45 ZUP) would signal flexibility while still prioritizing my production cost. I’ll aim for **42 ZUP**—a slight premium that acknowledges urgency but protects my investment.

</message>
```
